/-- Generic 5-factor functor-image fold: a functor image of a collapsed chain. Stated
in abstract categories and applied by unification (the `X.Modules` diamond lever).
Project-local. -/
private lemma map_fold₅ {𝒞 𝒟 : Type*} [Category 𝒞] [Category 𝒟] (F : 𝒞 ⥤ 𝒟)
    {x₀ x₁ x₂ x₃ x₄ x₅ : 𝒞} {a : x₀ ⟶ x₁} {k₁ : x₁ ⟶ x₂} {k₂ : x₂ ⟶ x₃} {k₃ : x₃ ⟶ x₄}
    {k₄ : x₄ ⟶ x₅} {z : x₀ ⟶ x₅} (h : a ≫ k₁ ≫ k₂ ≫ k₃ ≫ k₄ = z) :
    F.map a ≫ F.map k₁ ≫ F.map k₂ ≫ F.map k₃ ≫ F.map k₄ = F.map z := by
  rw [← F.map_comp, ← F.map_comp, ← F.map_comp, ← F.map_comp, h]

/-- **Generic left-side assembly for the conjugated cocycle**: substitute the cast
coherence (`hcast`), migrate the counit decoration `Y` through the three trailing
comparisons by the naturality squares `nY1`–`nY3`, peel the chart component off the
front by `nX`, and fold the chart-level collapse `hfold`. Pure rebracketing in an
abstract category, applied to the concrete pullback chain by unification.
Project-local. -/
private lemma side_collapse_left {𝒞 : Type*} [Category 𝒞]
    {x₀ x₁ y₁ y₂ y₃ y₄ y₅ a₁ a₂ a₃ a₄ a₅ a₆ b₁ m₁ m₂ m₃ : 𝒞}
    {X : x₀ ⟶ x₁} {c₁ : x₁ ⟶ y₁} {c₂ : y₁ ⟶ y₂} {c₃ : y₂ ⟶ y₃} {w : y₃ ⟶ y₅}
    {c₄ : y₃ ⟶ y₄} {Y : y₄ ⟶ y₅}
    {F1 : x₁ ⟶ a₁} {F2 : a₁ ⟶ a₂} {F3 : a₂ ⟶ a₃} {F4 : a₃ ⟶ a₄} {F5 : a₄ ⟶ a₅}
    {F6 : a₅ ⟶ a₆} {F7 : a₆ ⟶ y₄}
    {F1' : x₀ ⟶ b₁} {X' : b₁ ⟶ a₁}
    {Y₁ : a₆ ⟶ m₁} {F7' : m₁ ⟶ y₅} {Y₂ : a₅ ⟶ m₂} {F6' : m₂ ⟶ m₁}
    {Y₃ : a₄ ⟶ m₃} {F5' : m₃ ⟶ m₂} {Z : b₁ ⟶ m₃}
    (hw : w = c₄ ≫ Y)
    (hcast : c₁ ≫ c₂ ≫ c₃ ≫ c₄ = F1 ≫ F2 ≫ F3 ≫ F4 ≫ F5 ≫ F6 ≫ F7)
    (nX : X ≫ F1 = F1' ≫ X')
    (nY1 : F7 ≫ Y = Y₁ ≫ F7')
    (nY2 : F6 ≫ Y₁ = Y₂ ≫ F6')
    (nY3 : F5 ≫ Y₂ = Y₃ ≫ F5')
    (hfold : X' ≫ F2 ≫ F3 ≫ F4 ≫ Y₃ = Z) :
    X ≫ c₁ ≫ c₂ ≫ c₃ ≫ w = F1' ≫ Z ≫ F5' ≫ F6' ≫ F7' := by
  rw [hw]
  calc X ≫ c₁ ≫ c₂ ≫ c₃ ≫ c₄ ≫ Y
      = X ≫ (c₁ ≫ c₂ ≫ c₃ ≫ c₄) ≫ Y := by simp only [Category.assoc]
    _ = X ≫ (F1 ≫ F2 ≫ F3 ≫ F4 ≫ F5 ≫ F6 ≫ F7) ≫ Y := by rw [hcast]
    _ = X ≫ F1 ≫ F2 ≫ F3 ≫ F4 ≫ F5 ≫ F6 ≫ F7 ≫ Y := by simp only [Category.assoc]
    _ = F1' ≫ X' ≫ F2 ≫ F3 ≫ F4 ≫ F5 ≫ F6 ≫ Y₁ ≫ F7' := by
        rw [reassoc_of% nX, nY1]
    _ = F1' ≫ X' ≫ F2 ≫ F3 ≫ F4 ≫ F5 ≫ Y₂ ≫ F6' ≫ F7' := by rw [reassoc_of% nY2]
    _ = F1' ≫ X' ≫ F2 ≫ F3 ≫ F4 ≫ Y₃ ≫ F5' ≫ F6' ≫ F7' := by rw [reassoc_of% nY3]
    _ = F1' ≫ (X' ≫ F2 ≫ F3 ≫ F4 ≫ Y₃) ≫ F5' ≫ F6' ≫ F7' := by
        simp only [Category.assoc]
    _ = F1' ≫ Z ≫ F5' ≫ F6' ≫ F7' := by rw [hfold]

/-- **Generic right-side assembly for the conjugated cocycle**: like
`side_collapse_left`, with three extra moves — the front comparison splits off a
source-bridge congruence (`hc₁`), the pair congruence inside the last factor commutes
out (`hex`), the three middle congruences merge (`hstack`) — and a trailing transition
factor `d₄` riding along. Pure rebracketing in an abstract category. Project-local. -/
private lemma side_collapse_right {𝒞 : Type*} [Category 𝒞]
    {x₀ x₁ s₁ s₂ y₁ y₂ y₃ z₁ z₂ z₃ y₆ e₁ a₁ a₂ a₃ a₄ a₅ a₆ b₀ b₁ m₁ m₂ m₃ : 𝒞}
    {X : x₀ ⟶ x₁} {c₁ : x₁ ⟶ y₁} {c₂ : y₁ ⟶ y₂} {c₃ : y₂ ⟶ y₃} {w : y₃ ⟶ y₆}
    {d₁ : y₃ ⟶ z₁} {d₂ : z₁ ⟶ z₂} {d₃ : z₂ ⟶ z₃} {d₄ : z₃ ⟶ y₆}
    {Cs : x₁ ⟶ s₁} {c₁' : s₁ ⟶ s₂} {ce₁ : s₂ ⟶ y₁} {ce₂ : y₂ ⟶ e₁} {c₃' : e₁ ⟶ z₁}
    {ctq : s₂ ⟶ e₁}
    {G1 : s₁ ⟶ a₁} {G2 : a₁ ⟶ a₂} {G3 : a₂ ⟶ a₃} {G4 : a₃ ⟶ a₄} {G5 : a₄ ⟶ a₅}
    {G6 : a₅ ⟶ a₆} {G7 : a₆ ⟶ z₂}

    {Y₃ : a₄ ⟶ m₃} {G5' : m₃ ⟶ m₂} {Z : b₁ ⟶ m₃}
    (hw : w = d₁ ≫ d₂ ≫ d₃ ≫ d₄)
    (hc₁ : c₁ = Cs ≫ c₁' ≫ ce₁)
    (hex : c₃ ≫ d₁ = ce₂ ≫ c₃')
    (hstack : ∀ {Z'} (zc : e₁ ⟶ Z'), ce₁ ≫ c₂ ≫ ce₂ ≫ zc = ctq ≫ zc)
    (hcast : c₁' ≫ ctq ≫ c₃' ≫ d₂ = G1 ≫ G2 ≫ G3 ≫ G4 ≫ G5 ≫ G6 ≫ G7)
    (nXpre : X ≫ Cs = Cs' ≫ X₁)
    (nX : X₁ ≫ G1 = G1' ≫ X₂)
    (nY1 : G7 ≫ d₃ = Y₁ ≫ G7')
    (nY2 : G6 ≫ Y₁ = Y₂ ≫ G6')
    (nY3 : G5 ≫ Y₂ = Y₃ ≫ G5')
    (hfold : X₂ ≫ G2 ≫ G3 ≫ G4 ≫ Y₃ = Z) :
    X ≫ c₁ ≫ c₂ ≫ c₃ ≫ w = Cs' ≫ G1' ≫ Z ≫ G5' ≫ G6' ≫ G7' ≫ d₄ := by
  rw [hw, hc₁]
  calc X ≫ (Cs ≫ c₁' ≫ ce₁) ≫ c₂ ≫ c₃ ≫ d₁ ≫ d₂ ≫ d₃ ≫ d₄
      = X ≫ Cs ≫ c₁' ≫ ce₁ ≫ c₂ ≫ (c₃ ≫ d₁) ≫ d₂ ≫ d₃ ≫ d₄ := by
        simp only [Category.assoc]

        rw [reassoc_of% nXpre]; simp only [Category.assoc]

    _ = Cs' ≫ X₁ ≫ G1 ≫ G2 ≫ G3 ≫ G4 ≫ G5 ≫ G6 ≫ G7 ≫ d₃ ≫ d₄ := by
        simp only [Category.assoc]
    _ = Cs' ≫ G1' ≫ X₂ ≫ G2 ≫ G3 ≫ G4 ≫ G5 ≫ G6 ≫ Y₁ ≫ G7' ≫ d₄ := by
        rw [reassoc_of% nX, reassoc_of% nY1]
    _ = Cs' ≫ G1' ≫ X₂ ≫ G2 ≫ G3 ≫ G4 ≫ G5 ≫ Y₂ ≫ G6' ≫ G7' ≫ d₄ := by
        rw [reassoc_of% nY2]
    _ = Cs' ≫ G1' ≫ X₂ ≫ G2 ≫ G3 ≫ G4 ≫ Y₃ ≫ G5' ≫ G6' ≫ G7' ≫ d₄ := by
        rw [reassoc_of% nY3]

        simp only [Category.assoc]

/-- **Generic final cancellation for the conjugated cocycle**: from the (flattened)
cocycle equation `h` whose left side carries three extra invertible factors
`a ≫ b ≫ c`, solve for the five-factor prefix against the right side decorated with
the three inverses. Pure rebracketing in an abstract category. Project-local. -/
private lemma final_cancel {𝒞 : Type*} [Category 𝒞]
    {x₀ l₁ l₂ l₃ l₄ l₅ e₁ e₂ e₃ r₁ r₂ r₃ : 𝒞}
    {L₁ : x₀ ⟶ l₁} {L₂ : l₁ ⟶ l₂} {L₃ : l₂ ⟶ l₃} {L₄ : l₃ ⟶ l₄} {L₅ : l₄ ⟶ l₅}
    {a : l₅ ⟶ e₁} {b : e₁ ⟶ e₂} {c : e₂ ⟶ e₃}
    {R₁ : x₀ ⟶ r₁} {R₂ : r₁ ⟶ r₂} {R₃ : r₂ ⟶ r₃} {R₄ : r₃ ⟶ e₃}
    {c' : e₃ ⟶ e₂} {b' : e₂ ⟶ e₁} {a' : e₁ ⟶ l₅}
    (h : L₁ ≫ L₂ ≫ L₃ ≫ L₄ ≫ L₅ ≫ a ≫ b ≫ c = R₁ ≫ R₂ ≫ R₃ ≫ R₄)
    (hcc : c ≫ c' = 𝟙 e₂) (hbb : b ≫ b' = 𝟙 e₁) (haa : a ≫ a' = 𝟙 l₅) :
    L₁ ≫ L₂ ≫ L₃ ≫ L₄ ≫ L₅ = R₁ ≫ R₂ ≫ R₃ ≫ R₄ ≫ c' ≫ b' ≫ a' := by
  rw [← h]
  simp only [Category.assoc]
  rw [reassoc_of% hcc, reassoc_of% hbb, haa, Category.comp_id]

end CastCoherence

input.new_string:
/-- **Left collapse of the conjugated cocycle** (the `(i,p)`-side): the chart pullback
of the `p`-th component, fed through the triple cast chain and the pulled-back counit,
collapses to the `(i,p)` base-change transport followed by the middle-bridge cast and
the ungrouping at `(τ, f_pq)`. Cast coherence (`pullback_cast_compat`) + counit
migration (naturality) + the pair collapse `glueChartComponent_overlap_collapse`,
assembled by the abstract-category rearrangement `side_collapse_left`. Project-local. -/
private lemma glueChart_legCompat_left (i p q : D.J) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q) ≫ D.f i p)).map
        (glueChartComponent D M g i p) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q) ≫ D.f i p)
        (D.ι i)).hom.app ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p)).obj (M p)) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (glueData_triple_square D i p q)).inv.app
        ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p)).obj (M p)) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))
        (D.f p q ≫ D.ι p)).inv.app ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p)).obj (M p)) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))).map
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (D.f p q) (D.ι p)).inv.app
            ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p)).obj (M p)) ≫
          (Scheme.Modules.pullback (D.f p q)).map
            ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (D.ι p)).counit.app (M p)))
      = (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q))
            (D.f i p)).inv.app (M i) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q))).map (g i p).hom ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q))
          (D.t i p ≫ D.f p i)).hom.app (M p) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (glueData_bridge_mid D i p q)).hom.app (M p) ≫
        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))
          (D.f p q)).inv.app (M p) := by
  have hpairp : (D.t i p ≫ D.f p i) ≫ D.ι p = D.f i p ≫ D.ι i := by
    rw [Category.assoc]; exact D.glue_condition i p
  exact side_collapse_left
    ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
      (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))).map_comp _ _)
    (pullback_cast_compat (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q)) (D.f i p) (D.ι i)
      (D.t i p ≫ D.f p i) (D.ι p) (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))
      (D.f p q) hpairp (glueData_bridge_mid D i p q) (glueData_triple_square D i p q)
      ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (D.ι p)).obj (M p)))
    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q))
      (D.f i p)).inv.naturality (glueChartComponent D M g i p))
    (((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))
      (D.f p q)).inv.naturality
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (D.ι p)).counit.app (M p))).symm)
    (((Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (glueData_bridge_mid D i p q)).hom.naturality
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (D.ι p)).counit.app (M p))).symm)
    (((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q))
      (D.t i p ≫ D.f p i)).hom.naturality
      ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction (D.ι p)).counit.app (M p))).symm)
    (map_fold₅ (Scheme.Modules.pullback (pullback.fst (D.f i p) (D.f i q)))
      (glueChartComponent_overlap_collapse D M g i p))

/-- **Right collapse of the conjugated cocycle** (the `(i,q)`-side, with `g_pq⁻¹`

  -- casts align on the nose (`final_cancel`). No case split on the degenerate pairs

  have hC2h := congrArg (fun e => e.hom) (hC2 i p q)
  simp only [Iso.trans_hom, Iso.app_hom, Iso.symm_hom, Functor.mapIso_hom,
    pullbackBaseChangeTransport, Category.assoc] at hC2h
  -- cancellation data for the three extra invertible factors
  have hcc : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (glueData_bridge_tgt D i p q)).hom.app (M q) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (glueData_bridge_tgt D i p q).symm).hom.app (M q)
      = 𝟙 _ := by
    simp only [pullbackCongr_hom_app_eqToHom, eqToHom_trans, eqToHom_refl]
  have hbb : (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp
        (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))
        (D.t p q ≫ D.f q p)).hom.app (M q) ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))
        (D.t p q ≫ D.f q p)).inv.app (M q) = 𝟙 _ :=
    Iso.hom_inv_id_app _ _
  have haa : (Scheme.Modules.pullback
        (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))).map (g p q).hom ≫
      (Scheme.Modules.pullback
        (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))).map (g p q).inv = 𝟙 _ :=
    ((Scheme.Modules.pullback
        (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))).map_comp _ _).symm.trans
      ((congrArg (Scheme.Modules.pullback
          (D.t' i p q ≫ pullback.fst (D.f p q) (D.f p i))).map (g p q).hom_inv_id).trans
        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback

## Blueprint dependency graph (leandag)

You can navigate the project's dependency graph read-only to find the
proven lemmas/defs your goal can lean on (with the exact `\lean{}`
names to apply) instead of re-deriving them. `archon` is on PATH;
`--json` prints parseable JSON to stdout (banner to stderr):

```
archon dag-query ancestors --node <blueprint-label-of-your-target>   # proven deps available to you
archon dag-query node      --node <label>                            # one declaration's status
```

It is a navigation aid only — the proof obligation and your blueprint
chapter remain the source of truth.

## Peer projects (read-only awareness)

4 peer project(s) are in scope (declared in `.archon/peers.yaml`). You may **read** their graphs to reuse infrastructure and avoid duplicating work — never edit them:

- **Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge** — `/home/archon/FormalizationProjects/Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge`
- **AV-structure** — `/home/archon/proj/AV-structure`
- **Cech-Cohomology** — `/home/archon/proj/Cech-Cohomology`
- **Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso** — `/home/archon/proj/Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso`

**Walk a peer's graph read-only** with `archon dag-query <verb> --project-path <peer> [--json]`. It reads the peer's cached `.leandag/dag.json` — it never rebuilds or writes anything in the peer. Verbs: `frontier, node, ancestors, cone, interface, overlap, unproved, sorry, gaps, needs-leanok, needs-lean, unmatched, leaves, roots, all`. E.g.:
```bash
archon dag-query node      --node <label> --project-path <peer> --json  # one decl: statement, proof, deps, status
archon dag-query interface --node <label> --project-path <peer> --json  # the API a decl exposes to its users
archon dag-query cone      --node <label> --project-path <peer> --json  # its full dependency closure
```
**Read their source directly (read-only)** once a query points you at a file: open the peer's `*.lean` for the Lean proof and `blueprint/src/**.tex` for the informal write-up, then port it here. Reuse the *strategy / structure*; the proof must still compile in THIS project (different imports, Mathlib pin, namespaces).

**Read-only, with one exception.** You may read any peer file and query its cached graph. The *only* write you may make to a peer is leaving a definition-improvement note: when reusing a peer's declaration forces you to reshape it (a more general statement, a cleaner signature), tell them with
```bash
archon peers note --target <peer> --lean-name <name> \
  --suggestion "<the better shape>" --rationale "<why — what you had to change>"
```
That appends to the peer's `.archon/inbox/<this-project>.yaml` and nothing else. Never write anywhere else in a peer, never edit their files, and never run a graph-mutating command (`leandag build`, `archon dag-graph`) against a peer (it would touch their `.leandag/`). All other writes outside this project are rejected.

Active prover mode: **prove**

## Your goal

Fill `sorry` placeholders with complete Lean proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.

## Workflow

1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it).
2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, and lemmas already found.
3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading it is often the fastest path forward.
5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
6. **Always save partial progress.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile, but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that erases real work.
7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.

**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.

## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)

The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:

- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").

The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.

## Protected declarations

Read `archon-protected.yaml` before touching any declaration. You may fill proof bodies of protected declarations but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.

## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)

`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:

- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.

## Avoid early termination

- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
- **Decompose**: break into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available) and solve each individually.
- **Hard bar is a minimum, not a ceiling.** If your objectives specify a "hard bar" (e.g. "add def + pin signature"), that tells you the minimum required — not where to stop. After meeting it:
  - If a recipe exists in `analogies/`, the blueprint chapter has a concrete proof sketch, or you can reason about an approach: attempt the proof body immediately.
  - If no recipe exists: formulate a strategy, attempt it, and leave a partial tactic block at the stuck point. A failed attempt with a `by ... sorry` is far more useful than a clean `sorry`.
  - A bare `sorry` with no attempt is only acceptable when the mathematical obstacle is explicitly named and at least one concrete approach has been tried and documented.
- **After closing all assigned sorries, keep going.** Scan your file for other open `sorry`s that share the mathematical context you've just developed. If you can see a direct path to any of them using lemmas or infrastructure you've already built, attempt them. Stopping at the assigned task boundary when adjacent work is tractable is artificial throttling — you have write permission over your whole file.
- **Comments are not progress. Code is.** If you can describe an approach precisely enough to write it as a comment or TODO, you can attempt it. Any `-- TODO: try X`, `-- could use Y here`, or `/- Next step: ... -/` you write is a sign you stopped too early — attempt X/Y first; only leave the comment if the attempt fails with a named error. The same applies to approaches proposed in task results: if you wrote it in your log as "a possible route", you should have tried it.
- **Autonomous decision-making within your file.** You are fully authorized to make every tactical and mathematical decision within your assigned `.lean` file without waiting for the planner. This includes: choosing proof strategies, adding helper lemmas, attempting adjacent sorries, selecting alternative approaches, deciding proof structure. **Never write "I'll let the planner decide", "I'll defer to the next iteration", or "this requires planner input" for any choice that is entirely within your file.** The planner is not on call; deferring wastes the entire context-loading investment of this session.

  The ONLY actions that require stopping rather than acting:
  - Modifying a *signature* of a protected declaration (you may still fill its proof body).
  - Editing another agent's `.lean` file.
  - Removing an existing public declaration that other files import.
  - Changing the type of an existing declaration in a way that would break other files.

  Everything else — including trying a strategy the planner didn't explicitly endorse — is your call to make and attempt now.

## When to stop

Stop only when progress is blocked by a specific, named obstacle in one of these categories:

1. A genuinely missing Mathlib ingredient — named precisely, attempted via informal agent, no workaround found.
2. A definition or signature that requires another prover's output — name the specific declaration and file.
3. A proof that requires fundamental restructuring of a protected or cross-file definition — name it.

"It seems hard", "the proof is complex", "I couldn't find the right lemma", or "the planner should weigh in on this approach" are NOT valid stop reasons. Document the specific obstacle and keep trying. Only stop when you can write: "I tried approaches X, Y, Z; each fails because [specific mathematical reason]; the informal agent suggested [route] which requires [specific missing ingredient]."

**Specifically banned stop phrases:**
- "I'll wait for the planner to decide."
- "This strategy should be confirmed by the next iteration."
- "I'll defer this choice to the planner."
- "The planner may want to reconsider the approach."

If you find yourself writing any of these: delete the sentence, pick the most promising route, and attempt it. The planner will correct the choice next iter if it was wrong — a failed attempt costs nothing compared to an idle session.

## Completion criteria

Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:

1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
2. Zero axioms introduced.
3. The file compiles cleanly.

## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof

When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature**. Three patterns are banned:

- **Reflexive-iso placeholder** — replacing `X ≅ Y` with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`.
- **`Classical.choice` around an explicit witness** — dissolving `Type` vs `Prop` friction without actually constructing the witness.
- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`** — discards the declaration post-elaboration, breaking blueprint cross-references.

**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, ship the typed `sorry` instead.

## When infrastructure is missing

Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before filing "Infrastructure missing":

1. **Use the informal agent** if an API key is available (check `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` first): call `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` with "Prove [goal] using only current Mathlib." Formalize whatever it suggests. If no key is set, skip to step 2.
2. **If the missing ingredient is ≤~100 LOC**: write it as a project-local helper — typed signature plus a genuine proof attempt. A partial proof body is better than a documented gap.
3. **Only after steps 1–2 are exhausted**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` with what you tried, why it failed, and the precise statement of the missing ingredient for the plan agent to assign in `mathlib-build` mode.

"Infrastructure missing" as a verdict requires: informal agent called (or key unavailable, documented), local helper attempted if in scope. "I don't see how" is not evidence — a failed attempt with `sorry` at the stuck step IS evidence.

When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.

**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.

## Proof style

- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is display-level only. Default to `change` when in doubt.

## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md

The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:

- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.

## LSP MCP tools

The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (`mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, etc.). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, …) — same tools, never shell binaries.

- Always invoke through your tool-call interface.
- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` — there is no such shell command.
- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If `success: false`, retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.

## Search protocol

1. `lean_local_search` first.
2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.

## Tooling traps

- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading. Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors. Rely on `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.

## Logging

Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):

```markdown
# Algebra/WLocal.lean

## wLocal_iff (line 45)
### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance
- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing

### Attempt 2
- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
- **Result:** RESOLVED
- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap

## Needs blueprint entry
- `Algebra.WLocal.helper_bijective` (line 78) — new helper, no blueprint block yet. Uses: `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective`. Reviewer/planner: add a `\label` + `\lean` + `\uses` entry so the 1-to-1 correspondence holds.
```

One section per theorem/lemma. Each attempt: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results. **Always include the `## Needs blueprint entry` section when you added any new declaration** (omit it only if you added none) — it is how the loop keeps Lean ↔ blueprint at 1-to-1.

## End-of-session handoff

**Before declaring done, run this self-review:**

1. Did I attempt every approach I wrote down as a comment, TODO, or "possible route"? If not, go back and attempt them.
2. Are there other open `sorry`s in my file I could attempt with the tools and lemmas I just developed?
3. Did I call the informal agent (or document that no key was available) before filing "Infrastructure missing"?
4. Is there any approach I thought of but skipped because "it would take too long" or "the planner should decide"? If it's in my file's scope, attempt it.

Only proceed to write the task result when all four answers are "yes" or "not applicable."

**Process lifetime discipline:** A prover lane stops by writing `task_results/<your_file>.md` and exiting normally. Do NOT run `pkill`, `killall`, `kill`, regex process cleanup, or any command intended to terminate Lean/Lake/shell/agent processes. Do not launch final verification in the background and then clean it up. Verification must be a blocking command whose result you wait for, or a clearly reported unfinished command. The runner/orchestrator owns cleanup after your report.

Before writing results:

1. Write `task_results/<your_file>.md` with current result, lemmas discovered, concrete next step, dead-end warnings.
2. Save all changes; ensure the file compiles.
3. **Write a `## Summary` section** stating: sorry count before → after; exact names of sorries closed; exact names of sorries still open and why; whether you attempted adjacent sorries beyond the assigned ones.
4. **Write a `## Why I stopped` section** — be brutally honest; the planner reads this. **Only Lean code changes count as progress** — a sorry closed, a helper lemma that compiles, a partial tactic block at a specific stuck point. Comments, docstrings, TODO notes, and task result prose do NOT count. If you wrote approaches in comments that you didn't attempt, say so explicitly:
   - `Real progress`: closed N sorries — name each one. State the sorry count before and after.
   - `Partial progress`: made measurable code progress (decomposed into sub-lemmas with proof bodies, closed one branch of a case split) but did not fully close. Name the specific advance and the specific remaining blocker. Decomposing a `sorry` into N named `sorry`s with only comments in their bodies is NOT partial progress unless the bodies contain genuine proof attempts.
   - `Cosmetics only`: changed formatting/comments/style with no proof progress — say so explicitly. **Renaming a `sorry` to a named helper lemma, adding a docstring, or extracting it to a separate declaration with a `sorry` body is `Cosmetics only` unless the body contains a genuine proof attempt.**
   - `Approaches written but not attempted`: you identified routes (in comments, TODOs, or log prose) but did not attempt them — name each one and why you stopped before trying. This is a valid but weak verdict; the planner will re-assign with a directive to attempt them.
   - `Avoided the goal`: attempted something adjacent but not the assigned target — explain why and what you tried instead.
   - `Infrastructure missing`: a specific Mathlib gap was the blocker — name it precisely, confirm the informal agent was called (or key unavailable), describe what it suggested, why it failed, and state the exact missing ingredient statement.
   - `Directive not followed`: explain which part of the planner's directive you deviated from and why.

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.

A long dispatch may be auto-backgrounded with a task ID — that's fine; wait for that task's result before acting. **Do NOT use the `Monitor` tool to wait** — it is non-blocking (returns immediately, "keep working"), so your turn ends and the subagents are abandoned mid-run. **Do NOT use foreground `sleep` / `until … ; do sleep … ; done`** — the harness blocks `sleep`. 61	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
62	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
65	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
66	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
67	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
68	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
70	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
71	
72	### Subagent permissions
73	
74	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
75	
76	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
77	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
78	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
79	
80	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
81	
82	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper.
83	
84	## Protected Declarations
85	
86	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
87	
88	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
89	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
90	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
91	
92	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
93	
94	Two semantic markers:
95	
96	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
97	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
98	
99	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
100	
101	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake build <module>` (it uses `lake build`, not `lake env lean <file>`, so dependencies are built and missing-import failures aren't spurious), and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents should NOT add or remove `\leanok` as routine bookkeeping — leave it to the sync. The one exception is the review agent: if it is **certain** the sync got a marker wrong (a clearly-finished proof left unmarked, or a marker present on something incomplete), it may manually correct it, but must explicitly surface and justify that override in its summary (see `.archon/prompts/review.md`).
102	
103	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
104	
105	## User Interaction
106	
107	The user provides hints in two places:
108	
109	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
110	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
111	
112	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
113	
114	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
115	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true or important at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
116	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
117	
118	## Agent Roles
119	
120	### Plan Agent
121	
122	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
123	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
124	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
125	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
126	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
127	- Curate `ARCHON_MEMORY.md` — the condensed, cross-iteration knowledge file injected (read-only) into every agent's prompt. Keep only durable facts that would surprise an agent reading the code fresh (dead-end tactics, files not to touch, Mathlib gap coordinates, protected invariants). Hard limits: ~10 bullets / ~600 chars; prune before adding. Do NOT duplicate what's obvious from the code or PROGRESS.md.
128	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
129	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
130	
131	### Prover Agent
132	
133	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
134	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
135	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
136	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
137	- A prover lane ends by writing its task result and exiting normally. **Do not kill Lean, Lake, shell, or sibling agent processes from inside the prover lane.** Do not run `pkill`, `killall`, regex-based process cleanup, or background-verification cleanup. If a verification command is still running, wait for its result or report that it is still running; process lifetime is owned by the Archon runner/orchestrator.
138	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
139	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
140	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
141	
142	### Subagents
143	
144	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
145	
146	### Review Agent
147	
148	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
149	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
150	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
151	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
152	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Normally leave `\leanok` to the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase; only override a marker the sync got demonstrably wrong, and justify it in your summary.
153	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
154	
155	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
156	
157	These run automatically between phases:
158	
159	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
160	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
161	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
162

1	# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GrassmannianQuot.lean — iter-078
2	
3	## isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map (was L2160) — **RESOLVED**
4	### Attempt 1
5	- **Approach:** Blueprint's stalk-wise route, entirely on Mathlib API: each point of the
6	  iso-locus has a neighbourhood `U` with `φ|_U` invertible; `Scheme.Modules.restrictStalkNatIso`
7	  shows restriction along an open immersion preserves abelian-presheaf stalks, so the stalk of
8	  `φ` is invertible at every locus point; the same comparison for the locus inclusion makes the
9	  restriction stalkwise invertible; `TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso` upgrades to
10	  an isomorphism of `Ab`-sheaves; `Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf` reflects isomorphisms;
11	  `restrictFunctorIsoPullback` transports `restrictFunctor` to `pullback`.
12	- **Result:** RESOLVED (~60 lines, no new sorries, verified by cold `lake build`).
13	- **Key insight:** Mathlib's `Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctor` + `restrictStalkNatIso`
14	  (AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Sheaf.lean) make the stalk argument essentially free — no
15	  module-sheaf stalk theory needed, only `Ab`-presheaf stalks.
16	- **Note:** the previously `sorry`-backed instance `isIso_pullback_chartLocus_map` and all the
17	  `chartMatrixHom`/`chartMatrix`/`chartMorphism` machinery downstream are now sorry-free.
18	
19	## chartLocus_isOpenCover (was L2147) — **RESOLVED**
20	### Attempt 1 (the Nakayama covering step, Nitsure §1)
21	- **Approach:** New pipeline, replacing the blueprint's stalkwise-surjectivity sketch with an
22	  affine-local splitting argument that needs NO stalk theory for sheaves of modules (which the
23	  SNAP notes record as ABSENT):
24	  1. **Epi between free sheaves on `Spec R` splits** (`exists_section_of_epi_free_spec`):
25	     conjugate through `AlgebraicGeometry.tildeFinsupp : tilde (ι →₀ R) ≅ free ι`; the fully
26	     faithful `tilde.functor` reflects the epi to `ModuleCat R` where epi = surjective
27	     (`ModuleCat.epi_iff_surjective`); `Module.projective_lifting_property` (free ⇒ projective)
28	     gives a module-level section; transport back through `tilde.map`.
29	     **Defeq check that makes this work: `CommRingCat.of ↥R = R` is `rfl`,** so
30	     `tildeFinsupp` applies literally over `(Spec R).ringCatSheaf`.
31	  2. **Matrix right inverse over `Spec R`** (`exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect_spec`):
32	     present the section by `matrixEndRect_unitEndSection`, compose with the new fully
33	     rectangular law `matrixEndRect_comp_rect`, conclude `M * G = 1` by the new
34	     `matrixEndRect_injective`.
35	  3. **Affine transport** (`exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect`): conjugate along
36	     `S.isoSpec.inv` with `pullback_conj_matrixEndRect`; pull the right inverse back along the
37	     ring iso `appTop`.
38	  4. **Pointwise (Nitsure):** trivialise `x.F` on an affine `W ∋ t` (affine-opens basis refining
39	     the local-freeness cover), present `ι_W^* x.q` by a matrix `MW` (epi: pullback is a left
40	     adjoint), get `MW * G = 1`; evaluate at the residue field of the stalk at `t`
41	     (`χ := residue ∘ germ`); `exists_isUnit_submatrix` (new, pure linear algebra:
42	     columns span by right inverse, `exists_linearIndependent`, basis cardinality,
43	     `Matrix.linearIndependent_cols_iff_isUnit`) gives a size-`d` subset `I` with invertible
44	     residue minor; the minor determinant `f0` has unit germ (residue ≠ 0 ⇒ outside the maximal
45	     ideal), so `t ∈ W.basicOpen f0`; on that basic open the minor matrix is invertible
46	     (`RingedSpace.isUnit_res_basicOpen` + `Matrix.nonsing_inv`), so the chart composite —
47	     identified chart-locally with `matrixEndRect (MW.submatrix id σ_I)` via
48	     `pullback_map_freeMap_pullbackFreeIso` and the new `freeMap_matrixEndRect` — pulls back to
49	     an isomorphism; transport along `(Wb.ι ≫ W.ι).opensRange` factorisation into
50	     `mem_isoLocus`.
51	- **Result:** RESOLVED (~600 lines incl. helpers, verified by cold `lake build`, 0 axioms).
52	- **Dead ends / warnings for future provers:**
53	  - `Epi → IsLocallySurjective` for `SheafOfModules` does NOT exist in Mathlib and the
54	    presheaf-image/balanced route is expensive — the tilde splitting above is the cheap
55	    substitute on affines; reuse it.
56	  - `simp only [Category.assoc]` and `rw` of `CommRingCat.hom_comp` FAIL silently across the
57	    `X.Modules`/`SheafOfModules` instance diamond in several spots (consistent with
58	    `gr-pullback-instance-diamond`); the fixes were term-mode assoc chains, `show` for
59	    rfl-equalities of ring-hom applications, and `inferInstanceAs (IsIso (… ≪≫ …).hom)` with
60	    explicitly assembled isos instead of `infer_instance` on compositions.
61	  - `matrixEnd M = matrixEndRect M` is `rfl` for square `M` (`matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect`), and
62	    `Matrix.submatrix_map` is `rfl` — used to make `matrixToFreeIso` apply definitionally where
63	    instance search across the diamond fails.
64	  - `Opens.isBasis_iff_nbhd` returns `TopologicalSpace.Opens ↥T`, not `T.Opens` — re-obtain
65	    through an ascribed existential or dot-notation (`W.toScheme`, `W.ι`) fails.

67	## grPointOfRankQuotient overlap compatibility (sorry at ~L3180 after edits) — PARTIAL
68	- **State:** still `sorry`, but the matrix heart of the proof is now FORMALIZED and compiling:
69	  - `pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap` — inverse form of the index-map intertwiner.
70	  - `presentedMatrix x j I hI` — the presented matrix of the quotient along any `j : P ⟶ T`
71	    where `j^* c_I` is invertible (generalises `chartMatrix`, which is the case
72	    `j = (chartLocus x I hI).ι`).
73	  - `matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix`, `matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor` — the presentation
74	    identities (the `J`-minor presents `j^*c_J` against `(j^*c_I)⁻¹`).
75	  - `presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis` — **Nitsure's `M^I = M^I_J · M^J`** over any such `j`.
76	  - `isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect` — converse matrix-unit bridge; with
77	    `matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor` (whose right side is a composite of isos when both
78	    chart composites are invertible) it yields `IsUnit (M^I_P)_J`, hence
79	    `IsUnit (det (M^I_P)_J)` via `Matrix.isUnit_iff_isUnit_det` — exactly the unit needed for
80	    the `IsLocalization.Away.lift` step.
81	- **Remaining for the sorry itself (steps 1, 4, 5 of the route below):**
82	  1. Both sides are `toSpecΓ ≫ Spec.map (classifying)` composites: `p1 ≫ chartMorphism I =
83	     P.toSpecΓ ≫ Spec.map (classifying_I ≫ appTop p1)` by `Scheme.toSpecΓ_naturality`
84	     (exact pattern of the proven `chartMorphism_rel`).
85	  2. **Change-of-basis matrix identity over the intersection `P`** (the jewel): with both chart
86	     composites invertible after pullback to `P`, the presented matrices satisfy
87	     `M^I_P = (M^I_P)_J * M^J_P`, by the morphism computation
88	     `matrixEndRect (M^J_P) ≫ matrixEnd ((M^I_P).submatrix id σ_J) =
89	      [Qr.inv ≫ q ≫ (c_J)⁻¹ ≫ Qd.hom] ≫ [Qd.inv ≫ c_J ≫ (c_I)⁻¹ ≫ Qd.hom]
90	      = matrixEndRect (M^I_P)` + `matrixEndRect_comp` + `matrixEndRect_injective`
91	     (all three now in-file). The J-minor identification
92	     `freeMap σ_J ≫ matrixEndRect M^I_P = Qd.inv ≫ (pb)c_J ≫ inv((pb)c_I) ≫ Qd.hom`
93	     follows from `pullback_map_freeMap_pullbackFreeIso` + `(pb)(freeMap σ_J) ≫ (pb)q = (pb)c_J`.
94	  3. `IsUnit (det (M^I_P)_J)`: the minor's `matrixEndRect` is the iso of step 2; present its
95	     inverse (`matrixEndRect_unitEndSection`) and use `matrixEndRect_comp_rect` + injectivity to
96	     get a two-sided matrix inverse, then `Matrix.isUnit_iff_isUnit_det`.
97	  4. Factor `p1 ≫ chartMorphism I` through `chartIncl` (= the away-localisation `Spec.map`):
98	     `IsLocalization.Away.lift` at `P^I_J ↦ det (M^I_P)_J` (unit by step 3); equality of the

100	     `IsLocalization.ringHom_ext` + `MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext` to step 2's matrix identity
101	     against `universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap` (GrassmannianCells, proven).
102	  5. Conclude with `Scheme.GlueData.glue_condition`.

content:
# AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GrassmannianQuot.lean — iter 079

## grPointOfRankQuotient overlap compatibility (was L3217) — PRIMARY TARGET

### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** The 4-step route from the iter-078 handoff comment, but with step (2)–(4)
  collapsed through a single reusable gluing lemma: `existence_chart_kpoint_eq` in
  `GrassmannianCells.lean` already proves `Spec.map (lift(f) ∘ θ̃_{I,J}) ≫ ι_J =
  Spec.map f ≫ ι_I` (glue condition + `chartTransition_comp_chartIncl` +
  `IsLocalization.Away.lift_comp`) — its `Field K` hypothesis is **unused**, so I copied
  it verbatim as `chart_point_eq` over an arbitrary `CommRing K` (it is `theorem … [Field K]`
  in a file I may not edit). The proof then reduces to:
  1. `comp_chartMorphism` — `p ≫ φ_I = toSpecΓ ≫ Spec.map (aeval (presentedMatrix x (p≫ι) I))`
     via `Scheme.toSpecΓ_naturality` + the new transport `presentedMatrix_comp`
     (`conjPullback_comp` ∘ `pullback_conj_matrixEndRect` through `matrixEndRect_injective`).
  2. `hgIunit` — the `I`-classifier sends `P^I_J` to `det((M^I_P)_J)`, a unit:
     `RingHom.map_det` + `universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix` (new: `aeval(M)(X^I) = M`)
     + `matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor` + `isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect`.
  3. `hkey` — `lift(aeval M^I_P) ∘ θ̃_{I,J} = aeval M^J_P` by `MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext'`;
     entrywise it is `imageMatrix_map_ringHom` (transport of `(X^I_J)⁻¹X^I`) +
     `presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis` + `Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul_cancel_left`.
  4. `chart_point_eq` + `comp_chartMorphism` on both pullback legs (J-leg re-based onto
     the common morphism with `presentedMatrix_congr` along `pullback.condition`).
- **Result:** RESOLVED. `chartMorphism_glue_compat` proves the `glueMorphisms`
  hypothesis; the def's overlap argument is now that lemma. File compiles via real
  `lake build` (exit 0).
- **Key insights / dead-end warnings for future provers in this file:**
  - **Bare `inv` / `infer_instance` are unreliable under the `X.Modules` diamond** even
    when the needed `IsIso` is a *binder of the same lemma* or a `haveI` in context.
    Symptoms: `invalid calc step … Trans Eq Eq ?m` (an unsolved instance mvar in a step's
    type) and `failed to synthesize IsIso (a ≫ b ≫ c ≫ d)` although every factor
    synthesizes individually. Fixes used: spell `@CategoryTheory.inv _ _ _ _ (...) hinst`
    explicitly (house style — now I know why), and build composites with
    `IsIso.comp_isIso' h1 (IsIso.comp_isIso' h2 …)` (explicit-proof variant).
  - **`rw [h]`'s closing `rfl` is reducible-transparency** and fails on goals whose two
    sides differ only in (proof-irrelevant or instance-path) hidden arguments — replace
    the calc-step `by rw [h]` with `congrArg (· ≫ g) h` (term-mode unifies at default
    transparency). Hit twice in the final calc.
  - `isIso_pullback_map_comp` could not be closed by naturality + `of_isIso_comp_right`
    (instance failure); the working route is `NatIso.isIso_map_iff
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp p a) c` (precedent: line ~2912).
  - **NOTE:** `grPointOfRankQuotient` was *moved* below the conjPullback transport block
    (it now needs those lemmas); name, statement, and body shape unchanged.

## represents left_inv / right_inv (now L4435 / L4440)

### Attempt 1
- **Approach:** Scoped the missing layers before writing code. Both laws need
  (a) `chartComposite (rqPullback ψ y) ↔ ψ^*(chartComposite y)` (bridge), (b) the chart
  loci of a pulled-back quotient vs the `ψ`-preimage of loci, and — taut-specifically —
  (c) `chartLocus (tautologicalRankQuotient) I = (theGlueData).ι I`-image plus the
  identification of the tautological quotient's chart presentation with `X^I`
  (`universalQuotient_restrictionIso` / glue-restriction machinery). (b)+(c) are each
  100–300-line proofs entangled with the GlueDescent restriction API.
- **Result:** PARTIAL — layer (a) is DONE: `chartComposite_rqPullback` (compiling, ~35
  lines, via `pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap`). Layers (b)/(c) not attempted in code; with
  ~140 s per build cycle and the session budget spent on the primary target, starting
  them risked leaving the file broken.
- **Next step (concrete, for the next prover):** prove
  `chartLocus_rqPullback : ψ ⁻¹ᵁ chartLocus y I hI ≤ chartLocus (rqPullback ψ y) I hI`
  (factor `U.ι ≫ ψ` through `(chartLocus y I hI).ι`, then `isIso_pullback_map_comp` +
  `pullbackComp` NatIso + `chartComposite_rqPullback`; conclude `le_sSup`), then the
  taut-specific layer (c) keyed on `universalQuotient_restrictionIso`.

## tautologicalQuotient_epi (L2470)
- Not attempted, per PROGRESS directive: pinned until GlueDescent closes BOTH sorries.
  GlueDescent closed one this iter (its remaining sorry moved to ~L2023 at last check),
  so the gate is still closed. Route comment left intact.

## Needs blueprint entry

All in `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GrassmannianQuot.lean`, namespace
`AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian` (reviewer/planner: add `\label`+`\lean`+`\uses` blocks
in `Picard_GrassmannianQuot.tex` so the 1-to-1 correspondence holds):

- `chartComposite_rqPullback` — uses: `pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap`, `rqPullback`,
  `chartComposite`.
- `isIso_pullback_map_comp` — uses: `Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp`,
  `NatIso.isIso_map_iff`, `Functor.map_isIso`.
- `presentedMatrix_congr` — subst lemma, no deps.
- `chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix` — definitional (`rfl`).
- `presentedMatrix_comp` — uses: `matrixEndRect_injective`,
  `matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix`, `conjPullback_comp`, `pullback_conj_matrixEndRect`.
- `presentedMatrix_submatrix_self` — uses: `matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor`,
  `matrixEndRect_one`, `matrixEndRect_injective`.
- `universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix` — uses: `universalMatrix`,
  `presentedMatrix_submatrix_self`, `MvPolynomial.aeval_X` (analogue of
  `universalMatrix_map_transitionPreMap`).
- `matrixMap_nonsing_inv` — copy of the *private* helper in `GrassmannianCells.lean`
  (`Matrix.inv_eq_right_inv`, `Matrix.mul_nonsing_inv`).
- `imageMatrix_map_ringHom` — non-private CommRing form of the private
  `imageMatrix_map_eq` in `GrassmannianCells.lean`; uses `matrixMap_nonsing_inv`,
  `isUnit_det_universalMinor`, `Matrix.submatrix_map`.
- `chart_point_eq` — CommRing generalisation of `existence_chart_kpoint_eq` (proof
  copied verbatim; the Field hypothesis there is unused). Uses:
  `chartTransition_comp_chartIncl`, `Scheme.GlueData.glue_condition`,
  `IsLocalization.Away.lift_comp`.
- `comp_chartMorphism` — uses: `Scheme.toSpecΓ_naturality`, `presentedMatrix_comp`,
  `chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix`, `MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext'`.
- `chartMorphism_glue_compat` — uses: all of the above + `chartLocus_isOpenCover`,
  `presentedMatrix_changeOfBasis`, `isUnit_of_isIso_matrixEndRect`,
  `matrixEndRect_presentedMatrix_minor`, `Matrix.nonsing_inv_mul_cancel_left`,
  `chart_point_eq`. Proof block of `def:grPointOfRankQuotient` should `\uses` it.

## Operational note for the planner

Running `lake build` while a sibling lane's *imported* file is mid-edit **deletes the
import's previous olean** when the rebuild fails (lake removes outdated artifacts before
compiling). This briefly left GlueDescent.olean missing, blocking both build and LSP for
this lane; recovered by a background retry loop until the sibling's file compiled.
Candidate ARCHON_MEMORY bullet: provers with cross-lane imports should expect transient
import breakage and retry, not hard-fail.

## Summary

- Sorry count: **4 → 3** (file compiles cleanly, `lake build` exit 0, no new axioms).
- Closed: the `grPointOfRankQuotient` overlap-compatibility sorry (old L3217) — via new
  lemma `chartMorphism_glue_compat` + 10 supporting lemmas (all compiling, listed above).
  `def:grPointOfRankQuotient` is now fully realized; `grPointOfRankQuotient_rel` and the
  `homEquiv.invFun` of `represents` now rest on a sorry-free inverse construction.
- Still open: `tautologicalQuotient_epi` (L2470, pinned on the GlueDescent keystone per
  directive — gate still closed, 1 sorry left there at last check);
  `represents.left_inv`/`right_inv` (L4435/L4440) — first bridge layer landed
  (`chartComposite_rqPullback`), remaining layers scoped concretely above.
- Adjacent work beyond assignment: `chartComposite_rqPullback` (toward the inverse laws).

## Why I stopped

`Real progress`: closed 1 sorry — the assigned PRIMARY target
`grPointOfRankQuotient` overlap compatibility (4 → 3 sorries), plus 12 new compiling
lemmas including one (`chartComposite_rqPullback`) that begins the `represents` inverse
laws. The two `represents` sorries remain because their proofs need two further
substantial layers ((b) chart-locus pullback comparison, (c) the tautological quotient's
chart-locus/presentation identification keyed on the GlueDescent restriction API); I
scoped both precisely and landed the first bridge lemma, but starting (b)/(c) with the
remaining session budget (~140 s/build iteration, context nearly spent) would have
risked a non-compiling file. No API key in env, so the informal agent was unavailable
(not needed — no Mathlib gap encountered; the two private GrassmannianCells helpers were
re-proved locally). `tautologicalQuotient_epi` untouched per explicit PROGRESS pin.

content:
2740	  (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}), and on each overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two
2741	  presenting matrices are change-of-basis related by the invertible \(I\)-columns minor,
2742	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
2743	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}); hence the composites \(T_I \xrightarrow{\varphi_I} U^I
2744	  \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with the chart immersions agree on overlaps after the
2745	  transition \(\theta_{I,J}\). By the cocycle condition (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) these
2746	  chart-local morphisms glue to a single morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
2747	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}); this glued morphism is
2748	  \(\mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(x)\), the local-to-global inverse of the universal
2749	  property: pulling the tautological quotient back along it recovers \(\langle \mathcal{F},
2750	  q\rangle\) up to equivalence.
2751	\end{definition}
2752	
2753	The two blocks below carry the chart data of a quotient through a base change
2754	\(\psi : T' \to T\); they are the bridge underlying the two inverse laws of the
2755	representability theorem.
2756	
2757	\begin{lemma}[Chart composite of a pulled-back rank-\(d\) quotient]
2758	  \label{lem:gr_chartComposite_rqPullback}
2759	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartComposite_rqPullback}
2760	  \uses{def:gr_chartComposite, def:gr_rankQuotient, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap}
2761	  For a morphism \(\psi : T' \to T\) and a rank-\(d\) quotient \(y\) on \(T\), the chart
2762	  composite of the pulled-back quotient \(\psi^{*}y\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}) is the
2763	  \(\psi\)-pullback of the chart composite of \(y\), conjugated through the free-pullback
2764	  comparison:
2765	  \[
2766	    s_I \circ (\psi^{*}y)
2767	      \;=\; Q_d^{-1} \circ \psi^{*}(s_I \circ y).
2768	  \]
2769	\end{lemma}
2770	\begin{proof}
2771	  \uses{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap}
2772	  Unfolding the pullback action, \(s_I \circ (\psi^{*}y)\) is the coordinate inclusion
2773	  \(s_I\) followed by \(Q_r^{-1}\) and \(\psi^{*}y\). The inverse free-pullback comparison
2774	  intertwines the index inclusion (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso_inv_freeMap}): sliding
2775	  \(s_I\) across \(Q_r^{-1}\) turns it into \(Q_d^{-1}\) followed by \(\psi^{*}(s_I)\),
2776	  whence \(s_I \circ (\psi^{*}y) = Q_d^{-1} \circ \psi^{*}(s_I \circ y)\).
2777	\end{proof}
2778	
2779	\begin{lemma}[The chart locus of a pullback contains the preimage of the chart locus]
2780	  \label{lem:gr_chartLocus_rqPullback}
2781	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus_rqPullback}
2782	  \uses{def:chartLocus, lem:gr_chartComposite_rqPullback, lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp,
2783	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
2784	  For \(\psi : T' \to T\) and a rank-\(d\) quotient \(y\) on \(T\), the \(\psi\)-preimage of
2785	  the \(I\)-chart locus of \(y\) is contained in the \(I\)-chart locus of the pulled-back
2786	  quotient:
2787	  \[
2788	    \psi^{-1}\bigl(\mathrm{chartLocus}(y, I)\bigr)
2789	      \;\le\; \mathrm{chartLocus}(\psi^{*}y, I).
2790	  \]
2791	\end{lemma}
2792	\begin{proof}
2793	  \uses{lem:gr_chartComposite_rqPullback, lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp,
2794	    def:modules_pullbackComp}
2795	  Let \(U = \psi^{-1}(\mathrm{chartLocus}(y, I))\). The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow T'\)
2796	  followed by \(\psi\) factors through the chart-locus inclusion
2797	  \(\iota : \mathrm{chartLocus}(y, I) \hookrightarrow T\), over which the chart composite
2798	  \(s_I \circ y\) pulls back to an isomorphism (\cref{def:chartLocus}). Hence its pullback
2799	  along the factoring composite is an isomorphism
2800	  (\cref{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}), and the pullback pseudofunctor comparison
2801	  (\cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}) together with the chart-composite bridge
2802	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartComposite_rqPullback}) identifies it with the pullback to \(U\) of the
2803	  chart composite of \(\psi^{*}y\). So \(s_I \circ (\psi^{*}y)\) is invertible over \(U\),
2804	  i.e.\ \(U\) is one of the opens whose supremum defines
2805	  \(\mathrm{chartLocus}(\psi^{*}y, I)\); taking the supremum gives
2806	  \(U \le \mathrm{chartLocus}(\psi^{*}y, I)\).
2807	\end{proof}
2808	
2809	\begin{theorem}
2810	
2811	\leanok
2812	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
2813	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
2814	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
2815	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
2816	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
2817	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
2818	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2819	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
2820	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
2821	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2822	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
2823	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
2824	  %  contravariant functor
2825	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
2826	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
2827	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2828	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
2829	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
2830	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
2831	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
2832	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
2833	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
2834	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
2835	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
2836	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
2837	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
2838	  \[
2839	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
2840	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2841	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
2842	    \qquad
2843	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
2844	      \;\longmapsto\;
2845	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
2846	  \]
2847	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
2848	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
2849	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
2850	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
2851	  universal family.
2852	\end{theorem}
2853	
2854	\begin{proof}
2855	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
2856	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
2857	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
2858	    def:grassmannian_functor, lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi,
2859	    def:grPointOfRankQuotient, lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover, def:chartLocus,
2860	    lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map, def:gr_tautologicalRankQuotient,
2861	    def:gr_chartMorphism, def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso,
2862	    lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism, lem:gr_chartComposite_rqPullback,
2863	    lem:gr_chartLocus_rqPullback, lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat}
2864	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
2865	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
2866	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
2867	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
2868	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
2869	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
2870	
2871	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
2872	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
2873	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
2874	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
2875	  the composite
2876	  \[
2877	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2878	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2879	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2880	  \]
2881	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
2882	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2883	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
2884	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
2885	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
2886	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
2887	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
2888	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
2889	
2890	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
2891	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
2892	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
2893	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
2894	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
2895	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
2896	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
2897	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
2898	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
2899	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
2900	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
2901	
2902	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
2903	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
2904	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
2905	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
2906	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
2907	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
2908	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
2909	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\) -- this is the overlap compatibility
2910	  \cref{lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat} -- so by the cocycle condition
2911	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
2912	  \(\varphi = \mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle) :
2913	  T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient},
2914	  \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). This morphism is the candidate inverse; that it
2915	  is a two-sided inverse is the content of the two laws below.
2916	
2917	  \emph{Existence (the law \(\mathrm{right\_inv}\)):
2918	  \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u\rangle \sim \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\).}
2919	  The equivalence is checked over the chart cover \(\{T_I\}\). Over \(T_I\) the
2920	  morphism \(\varphi\) restricts to \(\varphi_I\) followed by the chart immersion
2921	  \(\iota_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chartMorphism}), and the restriction of the universal
2922	  quotient sheaf along \(\iota_I\) is identified with the free rank-\(d\) sheaf by
2923	  the chart restriction isomorphism
2924	  \(\phi_I : \iota_I^{*}\mathcal{U} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
2925	  (\cref{def:gr_universalQuotient_restrictionIso}). Through this identification
2926	  \(\varphi^{*}u\) becomes \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I\), which by construction of the chart
2927	  morphism (\(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\)) equals \(q|_{T_I}\) in the
2928	  \(I\)-trivialisation (\cref{def:gr_chartMorphism}, \cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
2929	  The witnessing isomorphisms \(\varphi^{*}\mathcal{F} \cong \mathcal{F}\) so
2930	  produced agree on overlaps -- the class is determined by \(\ker q\), which the
2931	  trivialisations match -- and assemble to a single isomorphism over \(T\), giving
2932	  \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u\rangle \sim \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\).
2933	
2934	  \emph{Uniqueness (the law \(\mathrm{left\_inv}\)):
2935	  \(\mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u\rangle) = \psi\)
2936	  for every \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).} It suffices to check the equality
2937	  after restricting to each chart locus
2938	  \(\mathrm{chartLocus}(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u\rangle, I)\) of the
2939	  pulled-back tautological quotient. Over such a locus the composite of its
2940	  inclusion with \(\psi\) factors through the \(\psi\)-preimage of the tautological
2941	  chart locus, which is contained in that locus by
2942	  \cref{lem:gr_chartLocus_rqPullback}; the chart-composite bridge
2943	  \cref{lem:gr_chartComposite_rqPullback} identifies the chart data of
2944	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u\rangle\) with the \(\psi\)-pullback of the
2945	  tautological chart data, so the matrix presenting it there is the \(\psi\)-image
2946	  of the universal matrix \(X^I\). Hence the chart morphism classifying
2947	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u\rangle\) over this locus coincides with the
2948	  restriction of \(\psi\) into the chart \(U^I\) (\cref{lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism}).
2949	  As the chart loci cover, the glued morphism
2950	  \(\mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u\rangle)\) equals
2951	  \(\psi\) by the uniqueness clause of the glue-data universal property.
2952	
2953	  Existence and uniqueness exhibit \(\mathrm{grPointOfRankQuotient}\) as a two-sided
2954	  inverse of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u\rangle\),
2955	  so the latter is bijective.
2956	
2957	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
2958	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
2959	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
2960	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
2961	\end{proof}

2258	/-- A **rank-`d` quotient of `O_T^r`** on a scheme `T`: a sheaf of modules `F` on `T`,
2259	locally free of rank `d`, together with an epimorphism `q : O_T^r ↠ F`. This is the
2260	unbundled datum whose equivalence classes form the value of the Grassmannian functor. -/
2261	structure RankQuotient (r d : ℕ) (T : Scheme.{0}) where
2262	  /-- The quotient sheaf. -/
2263	  F : T.Modules
2264	  /-- The quotient map out of the trivial rank-`r` bundle. -/
2265	  q : SheafOfModules.free (R := T.ringCatSheaf) (Fin r) ⟶ F
2266	  /-- The quotient map is an epimorphism (surjective). -/
2267	  epi : Epi q
2268	  /-- The quotient sheaf is locally free of rank `d`. -/
2269	  locFree : SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank F d
2270	
2271	/-- Two rank-`d` quotients are **equivalent** when there is an isomorphism of the targets
2272	commuting with the quotient maps (equivalently, when the kernels of the quotient maps
2273	coincide). -/
2274	def RankQuotient.Rel {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} (x y : RankQuotient r d T) : Prop :=
2275	  ∃ f : x.F ≅ y.F, x.q ≫ f.hom = y.q
2276	
2277	lemma RankQuotient.rel_refl {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} (x : RankQuotient r d T) : x.Rel x :=
2278	  ⟨Iso.refl _, Category.comp_id _⟩
2279	
2280	lemma RankQuotient.rel_symm {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} {x y : RankQuotient r d T}
2281	    (h : x.Rel y) : y.Rel x := by
2282	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
2283	  exact ⟨f.symm, by rw [Iso.symm_hom, Iso.comp_inv_eq]; exact hf.symm⟩
2284	
2285	lemma RankQuotient.rel_trans {r d : ℕ} {T : Scheme.{0}} {x y z : RankQuotient r d T}
2286	    (h1 : x.Rel y) (h2 : y.Rel z) : x.Rel z := by
2287	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h1; obtain ⟨g, hg⟩ := h2
2288	  -- term-mode (the `T.Modules` def-diamond blocks positional category `rw`)
2289	  exact ⟨f ≪≫ g,
2290	    (congrArg (x.q ≫ ·) (Iso.trans_hom f g)).trans <|
2291	      (Category.assoc x.q f.hom g.hom).symm.trans <|
2292	        (congrArg (· ≫ g.hom) hf).trans hg⟩
2293	
2294	/-- The equivalence-of-quotients setoid on `RankQuotient r d T`. -/
2295	instance rqSetoid (r d : ℕ) (T : Scheme.{0}) : Setoid (RankQuotient r d T) where
2296	  r := RankQuotient.Rel
2297	  iseqv := ⟨RankQuotient.rel_refl, RankQuotient.rel_symm, RankQuotient.rel_trans⟩
2298	
2299	/-- The **pullback action** on a rank-`d` quotient: pull the target sheaf and quotient map
2300	back along `ψ`, re-presenting the source as the trivial bundle via `pullbackFreeIso`. The
2301	result is again an epimorphism (pullback preserves epis) onto a rank-`d` locally free sheaf
2302	(`pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank`). -/
2303	noncomputable def rqPullback {r d : ℕ} {T' T : Scheme.{0}} (ψ : T' ⟶ T)
2304	    (x : RankQuotient r d T) : RankQuotient r d T' where
2305	  F := (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).obj x.F
2306	  q := (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q
2307	  epi :=
2308	    -- fully explicit: the def-diamond on `T.Modules` blocks `Epi`-instance search, so
2309	    -- `x.epi` is threaded through `map_epi`/`epi_comp` by hand
2310	    @CategoryTheory.epi_comp _ _ _ _ _
2311	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv inferInstance
2312	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q)
2313	      (@CategoryTheory.Functor.map_epi _ _ _ _ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ) inferInstance _ _
2314	        x.q x.epi)
2315	  locFree := Scheme.Modules.pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank ψ x.locFree
2316	
2317	/-- The pullback action respects the equivalence relation, hence descends to quotients. -/
2318	lemma rqPullback_rel {r d : ℕ} {T' T : Scheme.{0}} (ψ : T' ⟶ T)
2319	    {x y : RankQuotient r d T} (h : x.Rel y) :
2320	    (rqPullback ψ x).Rel (rqPullback ψ y) := by
2321	  obtain ⟨f, hf⟩ := h
2322	  refine ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).mapIso f, ?_⟩
2323	  change ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map x.q) ≫
2324	      (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map f.hom
2325	    = (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map y.q
2326	  rw [Category.assoc, ← (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map_comp]
2327	  exact congrArg
2328	    (fun m => (Scheme.Modules.pullbackFreeIso ψ (Fin r)).inv ≫ (Scheme.Modules.pullback ψ).map m) hf
2329

2332	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian
2333	
2334	/-- The **Grassmannian functor** `Grass(r,d)` (`def:grassmannian_functor`): the
2335	contravariant functor from schemes to sets sending `T` to the set of equivalence classes
2336	of rank-`d` locally free quotients `q : O_T^r ↠ F`, acting on morphisms by pullback.
2337	
2338	The object and morphism assignments are complete; the functoriality laws (`map_id`,
2339	`map_comp`) are discharged — via the naturality of the pseudofunctor comparison isomorphisms
2340	`pullbackId`/`pullbackComp` of `Scheme.Modules.pullback` — through the free-sheaf coherences
2341	`pullbackFreeIso_id`/`pullbackFreeIso_comp`, which reduce by coproduct extensionality to the
2342	unit-level coherences `pullbackObjUnitToUnit_id`/`pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp`. Fully proved. -/
2343	noncomputable def functor (d r : ℕ) : Scheme.{0}ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type 1 where
2344	  obj T := Quotient (rqSetoid r d T.unop)
2345	  map {X Y} g := TypeCat.ofHom (Quotient.map (rqPullback (r := r) (d := d) g.unop)
2346	    (fun _ _ h => rqPullback_rel g.unop h))
2347	  map_id X := by
